questionBank.question: The nth partial sum of sum a_n is:
questionBank.explanation: By definition, partial sum adds first n terms.
questionBank.timeTip: Write first few terms to avoid confusion.

questionBank.question: Alternating series test requires:
questionBank.explanation: Need decreasing positive b_n and lim b_n=0.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Check monotonic decrease and limit zero.

questionBank.question: If lim |a_{n+1}/a_n| = L < 1, then series:
questionBank.explanation: Ratio test with L<1 gives absolute convergence.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Determine whether the series converges absolutely, conditionally, or diverges.
parts.finalAnswer: Converges absolutely
steps: Check absolute series sum 1/n^2.
p-series with p=2 converges.
Therefore original alternating series converges absolutely.
parts.scoring: 1 pt absolute series setup, 1 pt test, 1 pt classification.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Is the alternating series error after n terms at most the first omitted term?
parts.finalAnswer: Yes, when terms decrease to 0 in magnitude.
steps: Alternating series error bound applies to decreasing b_n -> 0.
Then |R_n| <= b_{n+1}.

parts.label: (9a)
parts.question: Jack is sick and takes a flu medication every hour. He takes 100 mg initially and each dosage drops by 20%. If he keeps taking the medication forever, how much will he have taken? Jill is also sick. Each dosage of hers drops by 30%. If she ends up taking half the amount of medication that Jack eventually took, what was her original dosage?
parts.finalAnswer: Use the answer key PDF in Materials for this worksheet.
steps: Show all steps on paper.
Confirm with the answer key PDF.

parts.label: (10a)
parts.question: The annual spending by tourists in a resort city is $100 million. About 75% of that revenue is spent again in the resort city, and of that amount, about 75% is again spent in that city and so on. Write the geometric series that gives the total amount of spending generated by the $100 million and the sum of that series.
parts.finalAnswer: Use the answer key PDF in Materials for this worksheet.
steps: Show all steps on paper.
Confirm with the answer key PDF.

parts.label: (11a)
parts.question: This is a square of side 4 inches. The midpoints of the square are the vertices of an inscribed square. More squares are inscribed using the same pattern forever. The perimeters of the squares represents a geometric sequences. a. Determine if the infinite sum of these perimeters converges and if so, find the sum. b. The sum of the area of the squares is also a geometric series. Does this series converge or diverge? If it converges, to what value does it converge? 6 In the following exercises, (a) write the repeating decimal as an infinite geometric series and (b) express the sum of the series as a reduced fraction.

10.1.title: 10.1 Sequences and Partial Sums
10.1.content:
LIM-7 · BC Sequences

Sequences

A sequence {an} is an ordered list. Convergence means an approaches a finite limit L as n→∞.

Series = sum of a sequence

A series Σ an is the limit of its partial sums SN = a₁ + … + aN. If partial sums approach a finite value, the series converges.

nth-term test

If lim an ≠ 0, then Σ an diverges.

If limit is 0, the series may still diverge — this test never proves convergence.

AP tip: Always state the name of any test you use.

10.2.title: 10.2 Geometric and p-Series
10.2.content:
LIM-7 · Benchmarks

Geometric series

Σ arn converges (|r| < 1) to a/(1−r)   (|r| ≥ 1 ⇒ diverges)

p-series

Σ 1/np converges if p > 1, diverges if 0 < p ≤ 1

Harmonic series Σ 1/n diverges — compare other series to these.

Integral test (idea)

If f positive, continuous, decreasing, then Σ f(n) and ∫f(x)dx share convergence behavior.

AP tip: Memorize geometric and p-series results; they are comparison/limit-comparison anchors.

10.3.title: 10.3–10.5 Alternating, Ratio, Absolute Convergence
10.3.content:
LIM-7 · Tests

Alternating series test

For Σ (−1)n bn with bn > 0: if bn decreases to 0, the series converges (possibly conditionally).

Ratio test

Let L = lim |an+1/an|. L < 1 ⇒ absolute convergence; L > 1 ⇒ divergence; L = 1 ⇒ inconclusive.

Absolute vs conditional

Absolute convergence ⇒ convergence. If Σ|an| diverges but Σan converges, the series is conditionally convergent.

AP tip: For alternating series remainders, the error bound uses the first omitted term under AST hypotheses.
